name: Finomore, Victor S.
recent_1: Rezai, A. R., Thompson-Lake, D. G. Y., D'Haese, P.-F., Meyer, N., Ranjan, M., Farmer, D., Finomore, V. S., Hodder, S. L., Carpenter, J. S., & Mahoney, J. J. (2025). Focused ultrasound neuromodulation: Exploring a novel treatment for severe opioid use disorder. Biological Psychiatry, 98(1), 56–64. DOI: 10.1016/j.biopsych.2025.01.001
recent_2: Mahoney, J. J., Thompson-Lake, D. G. Y., Ranjan, M., Marton, J. L., Carpenter, J. S., Finomore, V. S., Hodder, S. L., & Rezai, A. R. (2025). A case report of focused ultrasound neuromodulation of the bilateral nucleus accumbens for methamphetamine use disorder. Experimental and Clinical Psychopharmacology. DOI: 10.1037/pha0000793
recent_3: Greenlee, E. T., Hess, L. J., Simpson, B. D., & Finomore, V. S. (2024). Vigilance to spatialized auditory displays: Initial assessment of performance and workload. Human Factors, 66(12), 2627–2640. DOI: 10.1177/00187208221139744
recent_1_synopsis: This open-label safety-and-feasibility study delivered low-intensity transcranial focused ultrasound to the bilateral nucleus accumbens in patients with severe opioid (and co-occurring) use disorders, tracking craving, substance use, and neurological safety over a 90-day follow-up. It positions noninvasive neuromodulation as a candidate adjunct to behavioral treatment for addiction, with Finomore contributing the human-performance and neuro-monitoring expertise.
recent_2_synopsis: A first-in-kind case report applying focused-ultrasound neuromodulation of the bilateral nucleus accumbens to methamphetamine use disorder, a condition with no FDA-approved pharmacotherapy. It demonstrates the precision targeting of reward neurocircuitry as a translational neuroergonomic intervention.
recent_3_synopsis: Using a spatialized (3-D) auditory display, this vigilance study measured detection performance and subjective workload to assess whether spatial audio aids sustained-attention monitoring tasks. It extends classic vigilance-decrement research into the auditory and communications-display domain central to Finomore's military human-factors work.
seminal_1: Finomore, V. S., Matthews, G., Shaw, T. H., & Warm, J. S. (2009). Predicting vigilance: A fresh look at an old problem. Ergonomics, 52(7), 791–808. DOI: 10.1080/00140130802641627
seminal_2: Sawyer, B. D., Finomore, V. S., Calvo, A. A., & Hancock, P. A. (2014). Google Glass: A driver distraction cause or cure? Human Factors, 56(7), 1307–1321. DOI: 10.1177/0018720814555723
seminal_1_synopsis: This paper revisited the long-standing problem of forecasting who will sustain attention under monotonous monitoring conditions, integrating cognitive, individual-difference, and workload predictors of vigilance performance and stress. With roughly 160 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2026), it is one of Finomore's most influential works and a touchstone for the resource-theory account of vigilance.
seminal_2_synopsis: An early controlled driving-simulator evaluation of texting via a head-mounted wearable (Google Glass) versus a smartphone, showing the wearable mitigated—but did not eliminate—distraction-related performance costs. With roughly 130 citations (Google Scholar, mid-2026), it is a foundational empirical reference for wearable-display distraction and remains his most-cited Human Factors article.
expertise_overview: Finomore studies vigilance and sustained attention, with particular emphasis on the workload, stress, and cognitive-resource costs of prolonged monitoring tasks. A core thread is the design and evaluation of auditory, spatial-audio, multimodal, and communications displays to support detection performance in demanding operational environments. His neuroergonomics work uses physiological and neuroimaging measures (e.g., cerebral blood-flow velocity, transcranial Doppler, EEG) to index cognitive state and operator effectiveness. He also examines human-machine teaming and trust in automation, including studies of automated driving and parking. More recently his research has expanded into applied clinical neuroscience, applying transcranial focused-ultrasound neuromodulation to substance use disorders and brain-barrier modulation.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence: Google Scholar (user=QaRBSfAAAAAJ), ORCID 0000-0002-9884-1152, WVU Health Sciences directory profile (Rockefeller Neuroscience Institute, Human Performance and Applied Neuroscience), and ResearchGate. Disambiguation anchored by the four supplied Human Factors DOIs, all attributed to this author. Career affiliations span US Air Force Research Laboratory (AFRL) and West Virginia University. Citation counts from Google Scholar as of mid-2026 (profile total ~3,976 citations, h-index 34); seminal counts are approximate per Scholar. All five cited DOIs verified via Crossref (matching title/venue): 10.1016/j.biopsych.2025.01.001, 10.1037/pha0000793, 10.1177/00187208221139744, 10.1080/00140130802641627, 10.1177/0018720814555723. No unverifiable citations. Note: his single highest-cited paper is Rezai et al. (2024), 'Ultrasound Blood-Brain Barrier Opening and Aducanumab in Alzheimer's Disease,' NEJM 390(1), 55-62 (DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa2308719, ~300+ citations), but as a large multi-author clinical-trial paper it was set aside in favor of papers where Finomore's human-factors/vigilance authorship role is more central.
